Indian Democracy
Universal Franchise Bill → Law
Democracy in Action  •  Your Vote, Your Voice
The Power of Every Citizen's Vote

Universal
Adult
Franchise

Every adult citizen — regardless of gender, caste, religion, or social standing — holds the equal right to vote and shape the future of their nation.

🗳️  See How Laws Are Made ↓
18+
Minimum Voting Age
1 = 1
One Person, One Vote
0
Grounds for Discrimination
100%
Citizens Eligible
1

What It Is

🪪  🗳️
Age 18+

The legal voting age for all citizens. Anyone who turns 18 is eligible to register and vote in all elections.

Equality

No discrimination based on gender, race, caste, or religion — every adult voice counts equally.

One Person · One Vote

Every single vote carries identical value and power. No citizen's vote counts more than another's.

2

How It Works

📋  ✅  🗺️
Registration

Easy voter registration for all eligible adults through government portals, camps, and local offices.

Casting the Vote

Private and secure voting on election day at designated polling booths using EVMs or ballot papers.

Representation

Choosing leaders who voice our collective opinions, needs, and aspirations in Parliament and State assemblies.

3

Why It Matters

📈  🏛️  🤝
Strengthens Democracy

Ensures a government truly "of the people, by the people, for the people" — accountable to every citizen.

Fosters Change

Shapes national policy, social reforms, and creates lasting impact for future generations.

Promotes Development

Leads to social progress, economic prosperity, and equality — when everyone votes, everyone benefits.

"
The ballot is stronger than the bullet. — Abraham Lincoln
⚖️   Your Vote Elects Leaders Who Make Laws   ⚖️
Constitutional Process

How a Bill Becomes a Law

The journey of legislation through the Legislature & Executive

Legislature / Parliament
Executive (President / PM)
Both Houses / Joint
Enacted Law
Rejected / Returned
1
💡
Legislature — Drafting
Bill is Drafted & Introduced
A Member of Parliament (MP), Minister, or the Executive drafts the bill and formally introduces it in either the Lower House (Lok Sabha) or Upper House (Rajya Sabha).
First Reading
2
📖
Legislature — First House
Committee Review & Debate
The bill is referred to a Parliamentary Standing Committee for scrutiny. Members debate, propose amendments, and refine the text over multiple readings before a vote.
Passed by First House
3
🏛️
Legislature — Second House
Vote in the Other House
The bill is sent to the second chamber for its own readings, debate, and amendments. Both houses must agree on identical text — differences may trigger a Joint Session of Parliament.
Passed by Both Houses
4
📜
Executive — Review
Bill Sent to President / PM
The enrolled bill is transmitted to the Head of State (President), who has a constitutionally defined period to review, sign, or return it under Article 111.
President
/ PM
Decision?
✓ ASSENTS / SIGNS
✗ WITHHOLDS / VETOES
5
✍️
Executive — Assent
Presidential Assent
The President signs the bill into law. The date of assent becomes the official commencement date.
★
📰
Enacted
Published in the Official Gazette
The Act is published in the Government Gazette, assigned an act number, and becomes binding law across the country.
↩️
Executive — Veto
Bill Returned to Parliament
The President returns the bill with objections. Parliament may amend or override with a ⅔ supermajority in both houses.
🔄
Legislature — Override
Override or Abandon
Parliament overrides with a supermajority — assent is then deemed given. Otherwise, the bill lapses and the process restarts.
Process applies to parliamentary democracies (India, UK, Canada, etc.).
Details vary by constitution — e.g., India's Art. 111, US Art. I § 7.
